summary:
The Viking discovery and settlement of Greenland (c. 982–985 AD) by Erik the Red was the western frontier of Norse expansion in the North Atlantic — the colonization of the world's largest island (then partially ice-free in its southwestern coastal areas) by a community of Icelandic Norse who would maintain European settlement there for nearly 500 years, longer than European colonization of the Americas has yet lasted. Erik the Red, exiled from Iceland for manslaughter, sailed west and explored Greenland for three years before returning to Iceland to recruit settlers, reportedly choosing the name 'Greenland' to make it sound attractive.

Around 985 AD, approximately 25 ships (14 arrived) carrying perhaps 500–700 settlers established the Eastern Settlement (near modern Nuuk) and Western Settlement. At their peak, the Norse Greenland colonies had 2,000–3,000 inhabitants, two dozen churches, a cathedral, a bishop, and traded walrus ivory, furs, and live polar bears to Europe in exchange for iron, timber, and luxury goods. The colonies maintained contact with Iceland and Norway for centuries. Their mysterious abandonment in the 15th century — the Western Settlement abandoned c. 1350, the Eastern Settlement c. 1408–1410 — is one of medieval history's most debated mysteries, with climate cooling (the Little Ice Age), declining walrus ivory prices as African elephant ivory became available, and conflicts with indigenous Thule Inuit all implicated.
